summary: Jeff Atwood responds to controversy over Joel Spolsky's criticism of Robert Martin's SOLID principles, arguing that while design principles have merit, there's a dangerous tendency to expand simple guidelines into elaborate rulesets. He uses the Star Trek Ferengi—whose 285 Rules of Acquisition govern every transaction—as a metaphor for programmers who believe sufficiently detailed rules can prescribe every situation. Atwood contends that skills, experience, and judgment matter more than any static ruleset, and that following rules blindly is for novices under supervision. The core message: study principles, but never let them substitute for critical thinking about your work.
key_points: Simple guidelines are useful, but expanding them into comprehensive rulesets creates dangerous complexity
Programmers are systematically disposed to believe they can 'program themselves' for success with sufficiently sophisticated rules
Following rules blindly is for novices; experienced engineers craft methods on a project-by-project basis
The Ferengi analogy illustrates how exhaustive rulesets inevitably get bent and reinterpreted to suit needs
Guidelines should be studied and respected, but never substitute for judgment and critical thinking
money_quotes: Expanding from a few guidelines on a paint can into a detailed painting manual is far riskier.
Pretty soon you'll find yourself believing that every possible situation in software development can be prescribed, if only you could come up with a sufficiently detailed set of rules!
At what point do you stop having a set of basic, reasonable programming guidelines – and start being a Ferengi programmer, an imperfect manifestation of the ruleset?
I value the skills, experience, and judgment of my team far more than any static set of rules.
Following is for novices who are under active supervision. Instead, I craft methods on a project by project basis.
I take responsibility for my choices. That's engineering for adults like us.
There's also a very real danger of hewing too slavishly to rulesets.
Rules, guidelines, and principles are gems of distilled experience that should be studied and respected. But they're never a substitute for thinking critically about your work.
key_insight: Design principles are valuable distillations of experience, but believing you can prescribe every situation with sufficiently detailed rules is a dangerous illusion—critical thinking and judgment must always come first.
